ocr.text: Figure 1. (a) Interaction between numbers of trials completed (= n. times paw given) per condition and the
decision speed component. A higher score on the decision speed component is associated with a slower reaction
in the inhibition tests. (b) Interaction between numbers of trials completed per condition and the impulsivity
score obtained from the questionnaire. A low impulsivity score indicates that a dog is less impulsive whereas a
high score means that a dog is very impulsive. Raw data (points) and predictions based on the selected model
(colours: black = ET, red = FC, blue = QI, green = RI).
Fixed effects Estimate ± SE z-value P
Table 1. Effects of inhibition components on paw-giving behaviour in dyadic conditions *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01.
Fixed effects Estimate ± SE z-value P
Table 2. Effects of inhibition components and DIAS impulsivity score on paw-giving behaviour in unrewarded
Moreover, dogs with a slow decision speed stopped giving their paw earlier than faster dogs (see Fig. 2b). Hence
dogs that take a longer time to make a decision in the inhibition tests also react more strongly to reward inequity
than dogs that make faster decisions.
In accordance with our hypothesis stating that inhibitory control influences the expression of inequity aversion,
we found that, overall, more compulsive dogs, i.e. those that consistently stuck to their initial choices regardless
of subsequent feedback in the inhibition tests, were more motivated to participate in the task. Furthermore,
